Supporting a family is about more than just earning money, it's about being dependable, present, and committed to the well-being of the people who rely on you. While games like Rust can be an enjoyable way to unwind, spending excessive time in a virtual world can take away from opportunities to improve your family's quality of life and strengthen relationships.    A good approach is to focus on a few key steps. First, prioritize your responsibilities by ensuring work, household duties, and family commitments are handled before recreational activities. Second, spend quality time with your partner, children, or relatives by having conversations, helping with daily tasks, and participating in family activities. Third, work toward long-term goals such as improving your skills, saving money, reducing debt, or planning for the future. Finally, maintain a healthy balance by treating gaming as a reward after important responsibilities have been completed rather than as the main focus of your free time.    A strong family is built through consistent effort, reliability, and time invested in the people who matter most. The hours spent supporting loved ones often create far more lasting value than the hours spent raiding kids at 5am on a weekday.

Rust is an excellent game that teaches valuable life lessons:  Never trust strangers. Never trust friends. Never trust family. Never trust anyone who says "I'm friendly."  I bought Rust expecting a survival game. Instead, I received a full-time job with unpaid overtime. I logged in for "just 30 minutes" and suddenly it was 4 AM, my base was gone, and a 12-year-old was explaining why I deserved it.  The progression system is fantastic. Spend six hours farming resources, carefully building a base, and organizing loot. Then wake up the next morning to discover that your entire existence has been converted into someone else's rocket budget.  The community is one of the most creative groups of people on Earth. They will spend weeks planning an elaborate raid just to steal 14 sulfur and a pumpkin.  Performance review:  Graphics: Good. Gunplay: Great. Building: Excellent. Mental health: Not recommended.  After 430 hours, I can confidently say I hate this game.  See you tomorrow.

Rust is genuinely one of the greatest survival games ever made, and I do not say that lightly. This game is pure chaos, pure pain, pure comedy, and pure addiction all wrapped into one brutal masterpiece. There is no other game that can make you feel like an unstoppable warlord one minute and a homeless caveman begging for mercy the next. Rust is not just a game you play. Rust is a full psychological experience.  The best thing about Rust is that nothing is ever guaranteed. You can spawn on the beach with nothing but a rock and a torch, get chased by a naked man screaming into his mic, somehow survive, farm enough materials to build a starter base, and then two hours later you are deep in a snow biome fighting full metal AK players over an airdrop. Every single wipe feels like a completely different story. Some wipes you snowball instantly. Some wipes you get doorcamped by the most unemployed human beings on Earth. Some wipes you become the villain. Some wipes the server makes you the victim. That is what makes it so good.  The progression in Rust is insanely satisfying. Going from a stone hatchet and bow to furnaces, guns, explosives, electricity, turrets, compounds, and raids feels better than almost any progression system in gaming. Every item matters because you worked for it. Every gun feels valuable because you either farmed for it, crafted it, stole it, or risked everything to bring it home. Getting your first SAR, Thompson, or AK of the wipe feels like winning the lottery. Getting back to base with a full inventory of farm while hearing footsteps nearby is more intense than most horror games.  The PvP is another reason Rust is so legendary. The fights are messy, stressful, and rewarding. Positioning matters. Aim matters. Game sense matters. Confidence matters. Even with all the chaos, there is a real skill gap. You can tell when someone knows how to move, when to peek, when to fall back, when to bait, when to push, and when to just run for their life. Every fight feels personal because there is actual risk. You are not just losing a round. You are losing the kit you farmed, crafted, or stole. That makes every kill feel amazing and every death feel like a crime against humanity.  Base building is honestly one of the most underrated parts of the game. Rust lets you be creative in a way most survival games do not. You can make a tiny 2x1 shack, a bunker base, a cave base, a clan compound, a trap base, a roleplayer mansion, or the most disgusting honeycombed fortress known to man. There is so much depth in building that it almost feels like its own separate game. Learning how to protect loot, place doors correctly, hide TC, design peaks, add external TCs, set up turrets, and make your base annoying to raid is genuinely fun. Nothing feels better than logging on and seeing your base survived the night.  The social side of Rust is what makes it completely unmatched. This game has some of the funniest, weirdest, most toxic, and most memorable interactions in gaming. You will meet random nakeds who become allies for the whole wipe. You will meet neighbors who act friendly and then betray you the second they get a chance. You will meet clans that treat a video game wipe like a military operation. You will hear the most unhinged voice chat imaginable. Rust creates stories naturally. You do not need scripted missions because the players are the content.  Raiding is one of the most adrenaline-filled things in any game. The moment you start throwing satchels, rockets, or C4, everything changes. You hear counters coming. You hear doors opening. You wonder if the base owner is online. You wonder if you brought enough boom. You wonder if the loot is going to be insane or if you just wasted hours raiding a base full of pumpkins and waterpipes. The uncertainty is what makes it so good. A successful raid can change your entire wipe. A failed raid can ruin your night. That risk is exactly why it is exciting.  Rust also keeps getting better because the game is constantly evolving. The map, monuments, weapons, vehicles, events, boats, trains, oil rigs, labs, cargo, helicopters, electricity, farming, industrial systems, and so many other features give players endless ways to play. You can be a PvPer, farmer, builder, electrician, fisherman, roleplayer, trap base goblin, ocean rat, monument runner, clan leader, solo grub, or just a menace to society. The game gives you tools and lets you decide what kind of creature you want to become.  And honestly, Rust looks and feels way better than people give it credit for. The environments can be beautiful, especially during sunrise or sunset. Snow biomes, forests, deserts, beaches, and monuments all have their own vibe. Then five seconds later someone is shooting at you from a bush and the beauty turns into panic. That contrast is perfect. Rust can be peaceful when you are farming wood at night with the sound of the ocean nearby, and then instantly turn into a warzone because a naked with an eoka decided your existence was offensive.  Is Rust frustrating? Absolutely. Is it toxic? Definitely. Will you lose hours of progress to the dumbest thing imaginable? Yes. Will you rage? Yes. Will you say you are done with the game forever and then load it back up the next day? Also yes. But that is exactly what makes Rust special. The highs are so high because the lows are brutal. The game does not hold your hand, and that is why every win feels earned.  Rust is not for everyone, but for the people who understand it, there is nothing else like it. It is one of the only games where every wipe feels like a story you actually lived through. You remember the base you built, the neighbors you hated, the raid you barely won, the kit you lost, the snowball that changed everything, and the random guy who betrayed you after you gave him a bag. No other game creates memories like Rust.  Overall, Rust is a masterpiece of survival, PvP, betrayal, creativity, and pure chaos. It is painful, hilarious, stressful, rewarding, and insanely addictive. It is the kind of game that can ruin your mood and still make you call it peak gaming. Rust is not just good. Rust is legendary.

So I started playing 'Rust' at 4pm yesterday.    Within 3 minutes of my first time playing, a bear ate me. Then I watched two other players club each other to death with large stones, while completely naked, penises (penii?) dangling in the wind. Having managed to slay a small pig, I fashioned some clothes to cover my genitals, then went exploring.    Ate some uncooked meat and threw up all over the place. Made a small fire to cook the rest of my meat. Fashioned a bow and arrow out of some wood I collected, also a shirt and a pair of boots. Went about fashioning a small shelter out of some random crap laying around so I wouldn't have to sleep outdoors. I could have chosen from one of the many, many, ramshackle structures dotting the landscape. But those may or may not belong to/contain other players. Who may or may not be armed or clothed. I just wanted to peacefully live alone, so I stuck to the wilderness.    I slipped off a cliff and broke both my legs. I shouted out to anyone who would listen, asking what to do to fix my broken legs, I didn't understand the game and I needed help. A naked man came over, typed '??? i help' in response to my pleas, then bashed my head in with a rock and presumably looted my corpse.    Then I starved to death when I forgot to eat. Out of desperation I butchered a wolf that attacked me instead of running away, but didnt have time to cook so I ate raw meat again. Nope. Instead of just starving to death, I vomited, then starved to death.    Its now 7:25 am and many adventures later, I've decided to sleep in a small shack I made myself in the mountains. Don't dare risk making a fire, someone might see it at night and kill me for the tin of baked beans I got off a zombie I found wandering around a town that gave me radiation poisoning. Oh yeah, I'll probably die slowly because of the radiation, but thats a problem for later-me.    In a tiny hut on a mountain with my sleeping bag and meager belongings after 12 hours of playing straight, I can probably safely say I'm enjoying this game.

Buying Rust on PC remains one of the biggest gaming regrets I've ever had. Within minutes of spawning into my first server, any excitement I had completely vanished. Instead of being welcomed into a challenging survival game, I was immediately surrounded by some of the most hostile and toxic players I've ever encountered in an online community. Racial slurs were thrown at me nonstop, and players openly told me that people like me weren't wanted there. It wasn't the occasional trash talk you'd expect from a competitive game—it was relentless, personal, and completely over the top.    Every attempt to progress was crushed almost instantly. Groups of heavily armed players hunted me down repeatedly, destroying everything I tried to build before I could even get established. After hours of struggling just to survive, I ended up being captured by a large clan that decided I was more useful as entertainment than as another player. They trapped me, stripped me of everything I'd gathered, and forced me into hours of humiliating in-game labor while mocking and harassing me over voice chat. Every attempt to escape ended with another beating, another cage, and another round of insults.    The experience felt less like playing a video game and more like being thrown into a lawless wasteland where the most aggressive and cruel people were rewarded for making everyone else's experience miserable. What should have been an exciting survival adventure turned into an endless cycle of harassment, intimidation, and frustration. By the time I finally logged off, I wasn't angry anymore—I was exhausted. Rust didn't just waste my money; it consumed hours of my time and left me with one of the most miserable multiplayer experiences I've ever had.    I've played competitive shooters, survival games, MMOs, and just about every genre imaginable, and nothing else comes close to how overwhelmingly negative this experience felt. If I could go back in time and stop myself from buying it, I absolutely would.

Rust is the type of game that you will really enjoy if you have no life outside of a computer screen. If you can dedicate 8+ hours of your time every single day, you will be good at rust, and have a good time. If you have a life outside of rust however, with the responsibilities that follow? Do not buy this game. Do not waste your time on this game. You can spend all of your free time playing this game, and never be as good, or have as much stuff as the 17 year old greaseball neckbeard who plays this game like a job.     Another reason you might want to avoid this game, is the fact that the player base is easily the most toxic player base you will ever come across, in any video game. You can simply say you don't like something about a specific server, and 15 random teenagers with no real life outside of this game, will tell you to kill your self, and say that your children deserve death. This is not a joke. These "people" will sit on their roofs for hours upon hours, making sure that everything that moves is killed, even if they do not gain anything from it. They will sit outside of your door for hours, simply because you killed them once, or said something they didn't like. Steal something from the wrong clan, just trying to enjoy a game after work? Be prepared for a 10 man clan to place TC's  around your entire base, with auto turrets covering each of them, all of which prevent you from playing out of that base entirely.    Lastly, FacePunch caters this entire game to people who play with groups. If you do not play with a group, you are pretty much entirely screwed, unless you are willing to spend hours just grinding out stuff. This game is not meant for solo play, because everyone plays as a team, and FacePunch very obviously cares about the team's more than the individual players. The current progression system is not fair to solo players whatsoever, and if you try to play solo or in a small group with inexperienced teammates, your wipe is pretty much over from the start.    With all of this being said, as someone who once loved rust, I highly recommend that you DO NOT buy this game, or spend your life trying to enjoy it. Unless you are just as toxic as the rest of this community, and have no life outside of video games, this game is not for you. The developers don't care about you, the player base genuinely hates you, and most staff don't do ♥♥♥♥. On top of everything else that I said, there are cheaters pretty much everywhere, on nearly every server. Find one that is breaking rules but being clever about it? 9/10 times, they will not be banned.    If this prevents even one person from buying this game, it will have been worth the time I spent writing this review. It may be too late to return this game, but if I could, I would. I don't think I will ever play this game again, in its current state.

Facepunch is slowly changing everything that made the old game enjoyable. The new character models are absolutely ghastly and have none of the charm the old models and animations had. Not to mention the inclusion of handholding BS like team ID system, compass, live map showing all POIs, server event notifications and endless hints at how to play because modern gamers apparently can't figure out anything themselves. I started playing in 2016 and the sense of isolation/survival in a huge, post-apocolyptic world was one thing that made Rust special for me. There's none of that feeling now when you can just stop by an NPC shop and buy a horse, minicopter or gun.  There weren't any hints, you had to discover and explore things yourself. Not much point now with a live map that shows everything at once in real time. I think many of these changes were to appease big streamers who like to put their fists through their expensive keyboards when they die. It seems as though Facepunch doesn't know what direction to take this game anymore, so they're turning it into a weird mashup of mostly Rust with elements taken from Tarkov, Pubg and Scum.

Can be very fun in its prime environement, but usually is not there.    As most say if you have a job, dog, any relationship or any other hobbies then you are already going to be behind constantly.    From what I can see high population servers are locked behind a premium payment model, and the rest are low population other than maybe the first week of a wipe. My experience is on low pop since I refuse to pay premium monthly payments on any game other than my top faves I can only review from this perspective.    To me this game is 3 simulators rolled into one, a walking, rock mining, and inventory simulator. You will spend WAY to much time just walking from point A to B, and wondering where you can stuff more chests into your base (which is only "meta" if you watch and copy a YT tutorial). You cannot just sit down and play this game casually for 30-60mins, logging on basically demands a 3 hour time crunch into those 3 boring tasks.    Then after you do that and attempt to search for the fun in this game you will log off, only to log on the next day a realise everyone (on low pop) just waits for your base to be empty so then THEY can have the fun and reset you all the way back to square 0.    I can appreciate brutally difficult games, but to me rust just simply does not respect your time. Oh and my GOD this has got to be one of the most toxic communities you will ever play in, and I like to think ive been around the block on PvP communities.    TL;DR:  - %90 of this is just boring grinding or literally JUST walking.  - Players will just wait till your offline then reset hours and hours of your progress, instead of doing the fun option.  - Absolutely toxic/troll community that doesnt even seem like they want new people to play the game.

In my opinion, games like Rust, Vein, Scum, and DayZ are barebones and boring. Just glorified sandboxes. 7D2D did better, but their game also needs work. They need more NPCs, lore, and worthwhile quests instead of the same generic "clear this building" BS. For those who want to play a quality game, try Icarus.

the fact that a competitive game with such a massive playerbase has so many pay to win elements is absolutely disgusting. money needs to be made, I am well aware of that. but enforcing a pay to win structure into this type of a game is not morally, ethically, and competitively acceptable.   the game uses EAC, which by itself could imply linux support. yet proton EAC runtime is not allowed for this title. why? EAC supports has proton support, its just an opt-in feature. so, what do you lose by flipping the switch?   optimization is... bad. this is most likely just the engine issue. a single base can already consume a ton of CPU and RAM despite just being a building in the visual sense. the environment and large populations on servers does not really help with this either. GPU is not a decisive factor that much, CPU and RAM ultimately matters much more, as the game's nature pushes the unity engine to its limit. this is not something that could be fixed, sadly. unity sucks at multithreading, and uses suboptimal garbage collection. multithreading is a feature that unity supports, but it still largely relies on single core performance, as you cannot really distribute work very effectively. while increasing single core performance is much more difficult than just adding more cores, modern CPUs are still able to kind of achieve it by just using clever designs. however, it should be noted that it still doesn't mean unity's poor multithreading is something that will not be an issue in the future.

I give this game 2.8/5.  It is A LOT of creative fun, but my biggest gripe is how INCONSISTENT it is. You walk back 5-8 feet back to be safe, then take 2 steps to the right and it shoots you. The PvP is brutal; your first 500hrs are going to be defensive play, learning the ropes hoping you can gather and build fast enough before others max level 2 workbench and start raiding bases. It is the most extreme game of "when its good its great, when its bad its awful". Tons of fun, but VERY time consuming with plenty of frustration.

When I started playing before the recoil rework, and even shortly after said rework I would have left a more possitive review, as I feel that was prime Rust. However now with all the constant monthly patches and "graphical overhauls" this game has never run worse than it is now, even with higher end systems.     Now with the removal of the non-customizable playermodels and replacement of the old playermodels with these new bog standard UE5 "human_1.mdl" looking ones they are planning on adding where you can see every individual blackhead on their noses. Not only does this strip the character away from the game but how will this run on lower end systems at all? If the game cant run on lower end systems, why are they adding more higher fidelity models instead of optimizing the game to run at a consistant 60fps without any stutter.    In my mind if the Facepunch team doesn't start doing monthly optimization updates instead of throwing even more bloat into this game every month, don't buy this game.   If they worked on the foundation of the game instead of filling the cracks with glitter glue, they would have a solid game people wouldnt want to leave and could actually run on your average gaming PC.

Received a permanent EAC ban on Rust despite not actively playing the game and not even having it installed at the time. I checked my Steam security, authorized devices, and login history — no signs of compromise, and Steam Guard was enabled.  What’s most disappointing is that my support ticket was marked as solved without properly addressing my concerns. I understand anti-cheat policies are strict, but legitimate players should at least receive a fair review process before having a permanent mark attached to their Steam profile.  I’m no longer concerned about playing Rust again. My frustration is with how this permanently affects my Steam account reputation without meaningful support or transparency.

The cheating problem in this game has gotten out of control lately and it's seriously hurting the enjoyment for a lot of players.  When I lose my base, I want it to happen because someone actually raided me fairly — preferably while I'm offline. Instead, we're constantly dealing with hackers phasing straight into bases, destroying everything, and headshotting players through walls. It's not fun, it's not fair, and it's driving people away.  Please prioritize anti-cheat improvements. The current situation is making the game feel unplayable for legitimate players.
